text: The laparoscopic approach to the splenic flexure, as with the laparoscopic approach to all colon surgery, was initially performed using the traditional lateral approach with which we were familiar from open surgery.

But just as the medial approach to the right colon and the sigmoid colon has revolutionized laparoscopic colon surgery, similarly, the medial approach to the splenic flexure provides an elegant and efficient means of taking down the splenic flexure laparoscopically.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery while preserving the arch of Riolon, facilitating the full mobilization of the splenic flexure of the colon.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior border of the pancreas to achieve full mobilization of the splenic flexure during the laparoscopic colectomy.
The surgeon places a high right upper quadrant trocar to facilitate the medial approach to the splenic flexure, alongside the usual right lower quadrant working port.

It is my usual preference to begin any resection of a low rectal cancer or foreshortened sigmoid colon with an assessment of whether mobilization of the splenic flexure is going to be necessary. If possible, I prefer to perform this at the beginning of the procedure.

The patient is securely fixed to the operating table, which is tipped to the right side. The surgeon elevates the omentum over the transverse colon and packs the small bowel from the left side of the abdomen over to the right side, enhancing exposure of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior edge of the pancreas, positioned laterally to the ligament of Treitz, preparing for its division.

The surgeon begins mobilization by dividing the peritoneum below the inferior mesenteric vein, creating a dissection window underneath the vein. The dissection plane is identified high in the area, similar to the medial approach used for the right colon.
The surgeon continues the dissection using blunt techniques, carefully teasing apart the tissue planes in the medial approach to the right colon.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection, maintaining the correct tissue plane with minimal bleeding observed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, identifying three landmarks, and carries the dissection plane down to the level of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon extends the dissection laterally and cranially, pausing to observe the pulsations of the left renal artery, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the cranial mobilization by reaching the inferior margin of the pancreas. The lateral dissection is finalized as the descending colon becomes visible.
The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization by incising the peritoneum along the white line of Toldt, entering the pre-existing window created during the medial dissection.

The surgeon rapidly advances the dissection up to the level of the inferior pole of the spleen. Starting from the middle of the transverse colon, the omentum is detached from the colon, allowing entry into the lesser sac.

The surgeon identifies the posterior wall of the stomach to confirm proper positioning within the lesser sac. The dissection is extended laterally to connect with the previously mobilized lateral side.
The surgeon applies upward traction on the omentum to prevent tugging on the spleen, aiming to minimize capsular injuries during the dissection process.

The surgeon approaches the base of the transverse colon mesentery, carefully stopping short of the left branch of the middle colic vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at the inferior border of the pancreas and divided.

The surgeon divides any mesentery or fusion between the distal transverse colon and proximal descending colon, ensuring protection of the arch of Riolan and perfusion of the distal colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, allowing for the distal resection to be addressed.
